## Overview
Harish Ravichandar is an **Assistant Professor** at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads the STAR Lab (see https://star-lab.cc.gatech.edu/). His work sits at the intersection of **robot learning**, **human-robot collaboration**, and **multi-robot systems**. He focuses on developing principled, data-driven methods for enabling robots to acquire and generalize complex skills—ranging from whole-arm and bimanual manipulation to heterogeneous team coordination—often by leveraging *learning from demonstration*, model-learning techniques, and intent-aware control. Central to his research agenda are the <u>learning of stable nonlinear dynamics</u>, <u>intention inference for anticipatory interaction</u>, and <u>task allocation and scheduling in heterogeneous teams</u>—themes that recur across his body of work and lab projects.

---

## Research Areas
Ravichandar’s research spans several tightly connected areas in robotics and machine learning. He has advanced methods for *learning from demonstration (LfD)* and for representing motion skills as stable dynamical systems (e.g., contracting dynamical system primitives) that enable robust reproduction of point-to-point, periodic, and pose (position + orientation) motions ("Learning Partially Contracting Dynamical Systems from Demonstrations" (2017); "Learning position and orientation dynamics from demonstrations via contraction analysis" (2019)). He has contributed broadly to *human intention inference* and human-in-the-loop control—developing EM- and multiple-model-based estimators and integrating gaze priors for early goal prediction to enable anticipatory and safe collaborative behaviors ("Human intention inference using expectation-maximization..." (2016); "Gaze and motion information fusion for human intention inference" (2018)). On the multi-agent front, he has developed unified frameworks and practical algorithms for task assignment, scheduling, and resilient coalition planning in heterogeneous robot teams (e.g., STRATA, GRSTAPS, D-ITAGS), along with MARL benchmarking and generalization strategies for cross-team policy transfer. More recently, his work explores *dexterous manipulation* and model-based representations (Koopman operator methods, neural geometric fabrics, ImMimic/CIMER) to improve sample efficiency and interpretability for multi-fingered hands and cross-domain imitation.

---

## Notable Works
- "Recent Advances in Robot Learning from Demonstration" (2020) — a widely cited synthesis that shaped the field's understanding of LfD paradigms, trade-offs, and practical considerations for deploying LfD in real robotic systems.  
- Development of contracting dynamical system primitives and related model-learning approaches for stable motion reproduction and pose dynamics (e.g., "Learning Partially Contracting Dynamical Systems from Demonstrations" (2017); "Learning position and orientation dynamics from demonstrations via contraction analysis" (2019)). These works provide foundations for learning stable, reactive motion policies from demonstrations.  
- Unified and resilient approaches for heterogeneous multi-robot coordination and tasking, including STRATA ("STRATA: A Unified Framework for Task Assignments in Large Teams of Heterogeneous Agents" (2020)), GRSTAPS ("GRSTAPS: Graphically recursive simultaneous task allocation, planning, and scheduling" (2022)), and D-ITAGS ("D-ITAGS: A Dynamic Interleaved Approach to Resilient Task Allocation, Scheduling, and Motion Planning" (2023)), which address allocation, planning, scheduling, and robustness under uncertainty.

---

## Academic Background
Harish Ravichandar is an active academic at the Georgia Institute of Technology, where he directs the STAR Lab and leads projects at the nexus of robot learning, dexterous manipulation, and multi-robot systems. His publication record—anchored by a highly cited 2020 review and a steady stream of methodological and systems papers—has garnered substantial community attention (total citations ≈ 2206). His work demonstrates sustained collaboration across subfields (LfD, dynamical systems, intention estimation, multi-robot tasking) and engagement with both theoretical and practical challenges in robotics. Through the STAR Lab he mentors students and collaborates with industry and academic partners to translate algorithmic advances (e.g., stable skill learning, intention-aware controllers, resilient heterogeneous coordination) into reproducible software and benchmark platforms.
